APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This work sits squarely within the scope of top NLP/ML venues: iterative self-learning for bilingual embedding mappings and lexicon induction addresses a core cross-lingual representation problem and compares to standard baselines (Artetxe et al., Mikolov et al., etc.) on appropriate datasets and metrics (bilingual lexicon induction accuracy, word-similarity correlations). The methodology and evaluation protocols are standard and the authors release code, but I note reproducibility concerns relevant to this community (hyperparameters are not reported and iterative/submatrix engineering choices can affect results), so while the paper is a strong fit, these missing experimental details keep it from being a perfect fit. | B(5): This paper is an excellent fit for a top NLP/ML venue: it addresses a core multilingual representation problem (bilingual lexicon induction) with a simple, practical self-learning framework that significantly reduces supervision (down to 25-word seeds or numeral lists) and matches or improves on strong baselines. The methodology and evaluations use standard datasets (Dinu et al., Europarl/OPUS, WMT/CommonCrawl) and accepted metrics, compare against the relevant prior art (Artetxe et al., Xing et al., Mikolov et al., etc.), and releases code, making it directly useful to the cross-lingual community. Limitations (iteration cost, memory engineering) are acknowledged, and the work speaks to both researchers and practitioners interested in low-resource and efficient bilingual mapping.

reviewer_a.justification: This work sits squarely within the scope of top NLP/ML venues: iterative self-learning for bilingual embedding mappings and lexicon induction addresses a core cross-lingual representation problem and compares to standard baselines (Artetxe et al., Mikolov et al., etc.) on appropriate datasets and metrics (bilingual lexicon induction accuracy, word-similarity correlations). The methodology and evaluation protocols are standard and the authors release code, but I note reproducibility concerns relevant to this community (hyperparameters are not reported and iterative/submatrix engineering choices can affect results), so while the paper is a strong fit, these missing experimental details keep it from being a perfect fit.

The methodology and evaluations use standard datasets (Dinu et al., Europarl/OPUS, WMT/CommonCrawl) and accepted metrics, compare against the relevant prior art (Artetxe et al., Xing et al., Mikolov et al., etc.), and releases code, making it directly useful to the cross-lingual community. Limitations (iteration cost, memory engineering) are acknowledged, and the work speaks to both researchers and practitioners interested in low-resource and efficient bilingual mapping.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions are stated clearly early (iterative self-learning loop, efficiency via orthogonal mappings and vectorized/submatrix computation). Notation and methodological description appear consistent enough to reproduce the loop, and related work coverage is thorough with code released. Presentation weaknesses: important experimental details (hyperparameters, stopping criteria and sensitivity/ablation protocols) are not reported in the summary, and the memory/submatrix engineering is noted but would benefit from clearer algorithmic exposition and illustrative figures or tables. | B(4): From the provided summary the paper is well-organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and the main contributions—an iterative self-learning framework that drastically reduces bilingual supervision and an efficient orthogonal-mapping implementation—are stated early. Notation and definitions appear consistent, and figures/tables/examples are reported as effective and informative. Writing quality is adequate, though a few practical-details (e.g., memory/submatrix handling and hyperparameter reporting) could be documented more clearly for reproducibility.

reviewer_a.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions are stated clearly early (iterative self-learning loop, efficiency via orthogonal mappings and vectorized/submatrix computation). Notation and methodological description appear consistent enough to reproduce the loop, and related work coverage is thorough with code released. Presentation weaknesses: important experimental details (hyperparameters, stopping criteria and sensitivity/ablation protocols) are not reported in the summary, and the memory/submatrix engineering is noted but would benefit from clearer algorithmic exposition and illustrative figures or tables.

reviewer_b.justification: From the provided summary the paper is well-organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and the main contributions—an iterative self-learning framework that drastically reduces bilingual supervision and an efficient orthogonal-mapping implementation—are stated early. Notation and definitions appear consistent, and figures/tables/examples are reported as effective and informative. Writing quality is adequate, though a few practical-details (e.g., memory/submatrix handling and hyperparameter reporting) could be documented more clearly for reproducibility.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper tackles an important practical problem—drastically reducing bilingual supervision for lexicon induction—by introducing a simple, iterative self-learning loop that empirically works with as little as a 25-word or numeral seed and provides comparable or better results than prior dictionary-based methods. From a reproducibility standpoint the approach is attractive (orthogonal mappings, vectorized induction, and released code), but the lack of hyperparameter reporting and the engineering caveats for submatrix similarity computation temper confidence in out-of-the-box adoption. Overall, this is likely to change how researchers and practitioners approach low-supervision cross-lingual mapping and will be widely built upon, though some effort will be needed to standardize protocols and sensitivity analyses. | B(4): This paper tackles the central problem of learning bilingual lexica with minimal supervision and shows a simple iterative self-learning recipe can succeed from extremely small seeds (as few as 25 word pairs or numerals), which directly challenges the assumption that large bilingual seeds or complex models are required. The approach is practically appealing—using orthogonal linear mappings and vectorized operations with code released—and thus is likely to change how practitioners approach seed selection and bootstrapping for many language pairs, including low-resource ones. Limitations around memory-heavy similarity computations and increased wall-clock time per iteration are acknowledged, but these are engineering constraints rather than conceptual barriers, and the method should stimulate follow-up work on scalability, robustness to typological distance, and downstream transfer uses.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles an important practical problem—drastically reducing bilingual supervision for lexicon induction—by introducing a simple, iterative self-learning loop that empirically works with as little as a 25-word or numeral seed and provides comparable or better results than prior dictionary-based methods. From a reproducibility standpoint the approach is attractive (orthogonal mappings, vectorized induction, and released code), but the lack of hyperparameter reporting and the engineering caveats for submatrix similarity computation temper confidence in out-of-the-box adoption. Overall, this is likely to change how researchers and practitioners approach low-supervision cross-lingual mapping and will be widely built upon, though some effort will be needed to standardize protocols and sensitivity analyses.

Limitations around memory-heavy similarity computations and increased wall-clock time per iteration are acknowledged, but these are engineering constraints rather than conceptual barriers, and the method should stimulate follow-up work on scalability, robustness to typological distance, and downstream transfer uses.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper clearly states its main claim (iterative self-learning substantially reduces required bilingual supervision) and supports it with experiments across multiple datasets and competitive baselines, and uses a correct analytical orthogonal mapping (Artetxe et al.) in a vectorized implementation. However, important reproducibility details are missing (no reported hyperparameters, stopping criteria, seed/variance analyses) and practical engineering choices (submatrix batching) are described without evidence they do not bias results; also statistical significance testing is not mentioned. These are minor but meaningful gaps that do not overturn the core empirical findings but limit confidence in robustness and exact reproducibility. | B(4): The paper's main empirical claim—that iterative self-learning with an orthogonal mapping can dramatically reduce bilingual supervision and match or exceed prior dictionary-based methods—is well supported by experiments across multiple datasets and strong baselines (including Artetxe et al. and Xing et al.). The use of an analytical orthogonal solution is standard and appears correctly applied; no problematic derivations are reported. However, the experimental section omits hyperparameter details and statistical significance testing, and engineering constraints around large similarity matrices are noted but not fully analyzed, which are minor but important gaps for reproducibility and assessing robustness across low-resource/typologically distant pairs. Overall the results justify the conclusions, with these caveats limiting a technically flawless rating.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper clearly states its main claim (iterative self-learning substantially reduces required bilingual supervision) and supports it with experiments across multiple datasets and competitive baselines, and uses a correct analytical orthogonal mapping (Artetxe et al.) in a vectorized implementation. However, important reproducibility details are missing (no reported hyperparameters, stopping criteria, seed/variance analyses) and practical engineering choices (submatrix batching) are described without evidence they do not bias results; also statistical significance testing is not mentioned. These are minor but meaningful gaps that do not overturn the core empirical findings but limit confidence in robustness and exact reproducibility.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main empirical claim—that iterative self-learning with an orthogonal mapping can dramatically reduce bilingual supervision and match or exceed prior dictionary-based methods—is well supported by experiments across multiple datasets and strong baselines (including Artetxe et al. and Xing et al.). The use of an analytical orthogonal solution is standard and appears correctly applied; no problematic derivations are reported. However, the experimental section omits hyperparameter details and statistical significance testing, and engineering constraints around large similarity matrices are noted but not fully analyzed, which are minor but important gaps for reproducibility and assessing robustness across low-resource/typologically distant pairs. Overall the results justify the conclusions, with these caveats limiting a technically flawless rating.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The experiments are broad and compare against a strong set of baselines across multiple datasets (En–It, Europarl OPUS pairs, WMT Common Crawl, RG-65, WordSim353), supporting the main claims about low-supervision effectiveness. However, key reproducibility details are missing (hyperparameters not reported) and the paper lacks thorough ablations/sensitivity analyses (e.g., seed size, initialization, stopping criteria, and submatrix batching effects), which I expect to validate robustness. Related work coverage appears thorough and limitations (runtime scaling, memory/submatrix engineering, coverage issues for some pre-trained embeddings) are acknowledged. Given code release mitigates some concerns, overall the experimental evaluation is solid but falls short of fully reproducible and exhaustive analysis. | B(4): The experiments are fairly comprehensive: multiple language pairs/datasets (EN–IT, Europarl OPUS pairs, Common Crawl, RG-65, WordSim353) and strong baselines are compared, and the method is evaluated under extremely low-resource seeds (25-word seed and numeral lists). The paper reports some sensitivity (seed-size experiments) but lacks detailed hyperparameter reporting and thorough ablations (e.g., impact of nearest-neighbor choice, normalization strategies, or convergence behavior across typologically distant pairs). Related work is treated thoroughly and limitations (training-time increase, memory/submatrix engineering, embedding coverage issues) are discussed, and code is available, which aids reproducibility despite missing hyperparameter detail.

Related work coverage appears thorough and limitations (runtime scaling, memory/submatrix engineering, coverage issues for some pre-trained embeddings) are acknowledged. Given code release mitigates some concerns, overall the experimental evaluation is solid but falls short of fully reproducible and exhaustive analysis.

The paper reports some sensitivity (seed-size experiments) but lacks detailed hyperparameter reporting and thorough ablations (e.g., impact of nearest-neighbor choice, normalization strategies, or convergence behavior across typologically distant pairs). Related work is treated thoroughly and limitations (training-time increase, memory/submatrix engineering, embedding coverage issues) are discussed, and code is available, which aids reproducibility despite missing hyperparameter detail.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares to a broad set of established baselines (Mikolov et al., Xing et al., Artetxe et al., Zhang et al., Luong et al., Lazaridou et al., BilBOWA, Upadhyay et al.) across multiple datasets and standard metrics, which indicates strong empirical grounding. However, important reproducibility details are missing (hyperparameters not reported) and the authors note coverage issues with some pretrained embeddings that complicate direct comparison; these limit confidence that all comparisons are fully fair. Code release and use of common evaluation metrics mitigate some concerns, so overall the empirical comparison is good but not fully rigorous. | B(5): The paper benchmarks against the major and recent baselines in cross-lingual embedding and lexicon induction (Mikolov et al., Xing et al., Zhang et al., Artetxe et al., Luong et al., Lazaridou et al., BilBOWA, Upadhyay et al.), uses standard datasets (Dinu et al. English–Italian, OPUS/Europarl-derived dictionaries, WMT/Common Crawl subsets) and common metrics (accuracy, correlation), and reports code. The authors explicitly discuss practical limitations and dataset/embedding coverage issues and situate their iterative self-learning approach relative to prior orthogonal-mapping and self-learning work (notably Artetxe et al.), indicating fair comparisons and acknowledgement of cases where prior methods are comparable. Overall the comparisons are comprehensive, fair, and the key competing approaches are cited and analyzed.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares to a broad set of established baselines (Mikolov et al., Xing et al., Artetxe et al., Zhang et al., Luong et al., Lazaridou et al., BilBOWA, Upadhyay et al.) across multiple datasets and standard metrics, which indicates strong empirical grounding. However, important reproducibility details are missing (hyperparameters not reported) and the authors note coverage issues with some pretrained embeddings that complicate direct comparison; these limit confidence that all comparisons are fully fair. Code release and use of common evaluation metrics mitigate some concerns, so overall the empirical comparison is good but not fully rigorous.

English–Italian, OPUS/Europarl-derived dictionaries, WMT/Common Crawl subsets) and common metrics (accuracy, correlation), and reports code. The authors explicitly discuss practical limitations and dataset/embedding coverage issues and situate their iterative self-learning approach relative to prior orthogonal-mapping and self-learning work (notably Artetxe et al.), indicating fair comparisons and acknowledgement of cases where prior methods are comparable. Overall the comparisons are comprehensive, fair, and the key competing approaches are cited and analyzed.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The proposed iterative self-learning loop for improving orthogonal bilingual mappings by using the model’s own induced dictionary is a useful practical idea but represents an incremental advance rather than a fundamentally new paradigm. The core components (orthogonal linear mappings, nearest-neighbor induction, and analytical mapping solutions) build directly on prior work (e.g. Artetxe et al., Xing et al., Mikolov et al.), and the novelty lies mainly in the combination and engineering (iteration, submatrix vectorization) plus the low-supervision demonstration (25-word seed/numerals). From a reproducible-methodology perspective this would likely inform practitioners, but it would not surprise an expert as a highly original conceptual contribution. | B(4): The paper applies an iterative self-learning (bootstrapping) loop to bilingual embedding mapping and dictionary induction, demonstrating that extremely small supervision (as few as a 25-word seed or numerals) suffices—a clear practical departure from prior supervised mapping work (e.g., Artetxe et al., Mikolov et al.). While self-training and orthogonal mapping are known ingredients, the specific combination, the engineering to scale induction (vectorized/submatrix similarity computations), and the empirical claim of drastically reduced supervision constitute a notable and clearly differentiated contribution rather than a trivial recombination. An expert would be informed by the simplicity and effectiveness of this approach, though it is not a radical theoretical departure from existing mapping/self-learning literature.

reviewer_a.justification: The proposed iterative self-learning loop for improving orthogonal bilingual mappings by using the model’s own induced dictionary is a useful practical idea but represents an incremental advance rather than a fundamentally new paradigm. The core components (orthogonal linear mappings, nearest-neighbor induction, and analytical mapping solutions) build directly on prior work (e.g. Artetxe et al., Xing et al., Mikolov et al.), and the novelty lies mainly in the combination and engineering (iteration, submatrix vectorization) plus the low-supervision demonstration (25-word seed/numerals). From a reproducible-methodology perspective this would likely inform practitioners, but it would not surprise an expert as a highly original conceptual contribution.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper applies an iterative self-learning (bootstrapping) loop to bilingual embedding mapping and dictionary induction, demonstrating that extremely small supervision (as few as a 25-word seed or numerals) suffices—a clear practical departure from prior supervised mapping work (e.g., Artetxe et al., Mikolov et al.). While self-training and orthogonal mapping are known ingredients, the specific combination, the engineering to scale induction (vectorized/submatrix similarity computations), and the empirical claim of drastically reduced supervision constitute a notable and clearly differentiated contribution rather than a trivial recombination. An expert would be informed by the simplicity and effectiveness of this approach, though it is not a radical theoretical departure from existing mapping/self-learning literature.

main_claims: Introduce a simple self-learning framework that iteratively improves bilingual embedding mappings and induced bilingual dictionaries by using the model's own induced dictionary as new supervision.

The proposed method drastically reduces required bilingual supervision, working with as little as a 25-word seed dictionary or an automatically generated numeral list while obtaining comparable or better results than prior dictionary-based methods.

The method is efficient in practice by using an orthogonal linear mapping with vectorized matrix operations for dictionary induction and leverages Artetxe et al.'s analytical implementation for mapping learning.
_summary.methodology: Iteratively learn an orthogonal linear mapping from source to target embeddings using a seed bilingual dictionary, induce a full bilingual dictionary by nearest-neighbor (dot-product) retrieval on the mapped embeddings, replace the seed with the induced dictionary, and repeat until convergence; computations are vectorized and performed on submatrices to address memory constraints.
datasets: English–Italian public dataset by Dinu et al. (2015)
Europarl-derived dictionaries from OPUS (for multiple language pairs)
Common Crawl corpora used at WMT 2016 (for Finnish and others)
limitations: Enclosing the learning in an iterative loop increases total training time proportional to the number of iterations.
Computing the full similarity matrix XWZ^T can be too large to fit in memory, requiring submatrix computation and additional engineering to combine results.
Coverage problems in some pretrained embeddings make some comparisons difficult (noted for some external embeddings).
_summary.topic_area: NLP - bilingual word embeddings / cross-lingual lexicon induction

reviewer_a.name: Reproducible Methodologist
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML engineer with 8+ years building and evaluating large-scale NLP systems, focusing on reproducible experimentation and robust empirical comparisons.
traits: insists on clear experimental protocols, hyperparameter details, and code/data release to reproduce results
looks for thorough ablation studies and sensitivity analyses (e.g., seed sizes, initialization, stopping criteria)
checks baselines are correctly implemented and computational/space complexity claims are supported by measurements
notices practical implementation choices (vectorization, submatrix batching) and expects evidence they don't alter results
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Concentrates on methodological rigor, reproducibility, and whether the experiments and ablations convincingly support the empirical claims.

reviewer_b.name: Cross-lingual Impact Specialist
reviewer_b.background: Senior NLP researcher with a long-standing focus on multilingual representation learning and cross-lingual lexicon induction, tracking both theoretical advances and real-world applicability.
traits: evaluates novelty and how the method advances or complements existing bilingual mapping/self-learning literature
assesses practical impact across languages and resource conditions, including low-resource and typologically distant pairs
values clarity about limitations, failure modes (e.g., hubness, unsupervised degeneration), and comparisons to the strongest prior art
looks for discussion of downstream implications (e.g., MT, transfer learning) and whether small seed methods generalize beyond controlled settings
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Focuses on situating the work within the cross-lingual literature, judging novelty and potential impact across languages and applications.
